title: How a revision is recorded
question: When a past decision changes, how do we record the revision?
rationale: Every revision becomes its own decision with a decimal id derived from the original (d47 to d47.1 to d47.2), filed in its release's folder and linked back with 'supersedes'. The id makes the lineage scannable, the original file is never moved or rewritten, and a revision can finally carry its own options and release - at the cost of more files, which the viewer groups by lineage. Supersedes the d36 global-integer numbering rule.

options.label: Each revision is its own decision with a decimal id (d47.1, d47.2)

tradeoffs.note: the id itself shows it's a revisit of d47 (d47.2 = 2nd revision) - no need to open it
the v1.0 file is never moved or rewritten; the revision is a new file in the new version's folder, so version folders stay clean and the original is immutable history
a revision carries its own options, tradeoffs and release - a real node, not a {from,reason} footnote
one-time change: decimal ids, a filename convention, a 'supersedes' link and viewer lineage rows; retires history[] as the revision mechanism
every revision now spawns a file, so the log grows faster than inline notes - softened by the viewer clustering a d47.x lineage together

options.label: Keep revisions inline as history[] entries (the prior model)

tradeoffs.note: a history entry can't hold its own options or release; a revision can never be 'its own decision'

options.label: Separate decision, opaque link only (d55 supersedes d47)

tradeoffs.note: full node with its own options and release
the id 'd55' gives no hint it relates to d47; you must open it to see the link

options.label: Hybrid: only substantive revisions graduate to their own decision

tradeoffs.note: trivial edits stay inline; only big re-decisions spawn nodes
two mechanisms for 'a revision' - you must judge each time which to use, harder to explain and to automate
